commonIssues: Heat sensitive: stops flowering and setting pods once temperatures exceed about 29C (85F); grow as a cool-season crop in spring or fall.
Young plants tolerate light frost, but open blossoms can be damaged by freezing temperatures.
Powdery mildew, white mold, and Fusarium wilt; water at the soil line and avoid wetting foliage.
Pests: aphids, cutworms, pea/bean weevils, pea moth, slugs, and snails.
Avoid excess nitrogen; peas fix their own and high N reduces pod production.
Tall varieties climb by tendrils to about 1.5-1.8m and require netting, trellis, or pea sticks installed at sowing.
Container minimum of 35L reflects RHS guidance (about a 45cm-wide pot) for dwarf and mangetout types; container plants dry quickly and yield less than in-ground.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension, Growing peas in home gardens: full sun; ideal growing temperature 55-65F (13-18C); growth/flowering stops above 85F (29C); soak soil to at least 1 inch per week during the growing season; peas need less fertilizer than other crops (low-phosphorus blends); soil pH 6-7.5; about 60 days to maturity; tall varieties to 5 ft need trellises; problems include cutworms, aphids, powdery mildew, white mold.
University of Minnesota Extension, Growing peas in home gardens: ideal growing temperature 55-65F (13-18C); plants stop growing/flowering above 85F (29C); young plants survive light frost.
University of Minnesota Extension, Growing peas in home gardens: peas need less fertilizer than other crops; low-phosphorus blends suggested by soil test.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS), Peas / grow your own: sow outdoors March-June once soil reaches 10C (50F); minimum container pot size 45cm wide for dwarf/mangetout types; container peas need regular watering as compost dries rapidly; feed container peas with a high-potassium liquid feed (e.g. tomato feed) once a fortnight from flowering; tall varieties up to 1.8m need netting/canes; water well at flowering and again two weeks later.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS), Peas / grow your own: feed container-grown peas with a high-potassium organic liquid fertiliser (e.g. tomato feed) once a fortnight (every 14 days) from when flowers appear; in-ground peas generally need no feeding.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC, Garden Peas: full sun for maximum flavor and sugar; cool-season crop that suffers in summer heat; frost-tolerant foliage but blossoms cannot withstand freezing; ideal pH 6.0-6.5; 5-10-10 fertilizer at 3 lb per 100 sq ft if no soil test; avoid excess nitrogen; early varieties 55-64 days; support with netting, trellis, wires, or pea brush erected at planting; powdery mildew, Fusarium wilt, aphids.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC, Garden Peas: full sun required; spring planting windows by region (Piedmont Mar 1-Apr 5; Coastal Feb 1-Mar 15).

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC, Garden Peas: support peas with netting, trellis, wires, or pea brush; peas climb by tendrils. RHS notes tall varieties reach about 1.8m. Supports high vertical/trellis suitability.

Confidence note: schema requires Celsius numerics not stated verbatim by sources. heatStressC=29 converts UMN/Clemson's 85F threshold. coldHardyMinC=-4 is a conservative estimate of 'light frost' tolerance for young foliage (sources state young plants survive light frost and blossoms are frost-damaged, without a numeric low). frostSensitive=false reflects the foliage's cool-season hardiness, but blossoms are frost-sensitive (captured in commonIssues). Watering baseIntervalDays=4 and hotWeatherMultiplier estimate from UMN's '1 inch per week, soak soil' plus RHS container 'dries rapidly'; rainSkipThresholdMm=12 (~0.5in) is a conservative care-engine default. recommendedMinLiters=35 converts RHS '45cm-wide pot'; idealMaxLiters=60 is an estimate for larger/taller varieties.
